name: S'Embut
starting_point: Na Burguesa viewpoint (Génova). Follow the path past the radio station antennas, continuing toward Coll des Pastors.
equipment: Only helmet and lighting for a normal visit
description: A two-chamber cave at different levels, discovered on August 13, 2008. The upper chamber features unusual cone-shaped roots that are slowly calcifying into 'hairy stalagmites' from constant water dripping. The lower chamber is more richly decorated with 'fried egg' formations and shows signs of periodic lake formation. Animal bones including an equine skeleton are present.
full_description:
It is curious that a cave of these characteristics, composed of two chambers at different levels, sufficiently decorated and housing some interesting curiosities inside — among others, some eccentric stalactites — does not appear in the catalog of J. Encinas Sánchez S. 'Inventory listing of natural underground features of the Balearic Islands' 2006. But it is true, it does not appear, although that does not mean the cave had not been visited before August 13, 2008, which was the day when, soaked from head to toe in the company of A. Villar, my wife, I found it. The fact is that in the cave there is written evidence that it had already been previously visited by members of a speleological group from the island who, to set a 'good' example, left their mark in more than one place in the cave with black spray paint. We were also able to find a piece of ceramic inside, but once visually analyzed by J. A. Encinas, it was ruled out as having any interest.

Like many other caves and pits on the island, the cave came to light through a terrain collapse that formed a kind of dustpan or funnel, at the lower part of which the entrance to the cave proper is found. The approximate dimensions of this entrance are about 5.5 m wide by 1.20 m high, and it is oriented at 27° NNE.

On August 31, 2008, accompanied by J. Encinas and G. Herráez, we began the exploration, starting the descent into the interior of the cavity down a considerable slope for several meters until reaching a small landing where we will turn left to continue descending in a NW direction through a kind of gallery with a slope close to 30% at some points, where the ground is composed of loose stones and earth but without any danger. Soon a chamber appears before our eyes that is not excessively decorated, but once analyzed more carefully proved very interesting as it houses some curiosities inside. The approximate dimensions of the chamber are about 20 m wide by 13 m long and 10 m high. The floor of the chamber is far from horizontal as it is composed of a ramp that ends at the bottom of the wall. On the wall, at a height slightly less than one and a half meters, a horizontal line seems to indicate an ancient water level in this place, but since the cave contains a chamber at a lower level, it is difficult to understand how that mark would indicate the level of an ancient lake. Of course, another hypothesis is possible, namely that said mark on the wall, instead of indicating a water mark, was indicating the level of sediment stored before the continuation of the cave opened up and the sediment collapsed downward.

Some of the curiosities housed in this chamber are more than interesting, notably some cone-shaped roots that grow in it as if they were plants seeking moisture they cannot find underground. But the most curious thing of all is that on almost all of them, water drops continuously drip, and upon hitting the tip of the root, a kind of hollow has formed. At the same time, due to the high calcium carbonate content of the water, the roots are transforming into curious 'hairy stalagmites.'

As in many other caves and pits, a large quantity of bones appears before our eyes in this chamber, notably part of the skeleton of an equine. However, the search for ancient ceramics was fruitless, as we only found one piece of ceramic that belongs to a recent period.

Although formations are not too abundant in this chamber, it is not devoid of them. Noteworthy is a small corner at the back of the chamber, at a height of less than two meters, to the right of the 'sinkhole' that provides continuity to the cave. Worth highlighting are a couple of small 'balconies' as well as a beautiful flowstone next to a thick stalagmite. Three small columns in a line open an alternative passage for us to continue descending through the cave to the aforementioned 'sinkhole,' and if one day it becomes blocked — something that could happen at any time — using vertical progression equipment would allow us to continue advancing. But as long as this does not happen, it is best to descend through the 'sinkhole' down a slope that reaches 50% at some points; there is even a small drop that can be overcome without great difficulty.

The lower chamber, the most beautiful one, is very well decorated, with multiple 'fried eggs' spread across its surface. Additionally, at the time of my visit, I could see signs that at some point a small lake forms in this chamber. Despite the first visit to the cave having taken place in August, the cave is active in both this chamber and the upper one.

The continuation of the cave runs through a narrow well of approximately 8 m depth that can be accessed without the need for vertical progression equipment but lacks interest.

There are many caves and especially pits in the vicinity, so if we do not have enough to spend the day with a visit to this cave, we can go to any of them. The nearest cave of some interest known by Mallorca Verde is the Cueva Bandolera, located on July 2, 2008, by us and which is worth visiting. The coordinates of the cave are: 464196 E, 4379554 N. DATUM EUROPEAN 1950.
